items.q: Why do meanders migrate across a floodplain?
items.correct: Faster water on the outside of the bend causes erosion (river cliff); slower water on the inside causes deposition (slip-off slope) — over time the channel shifts laterally.
items.misconception: Meanders form because rivers flow around obstacles like rocks.
items.random_wrong: Meanders form because the river is going downhill very steeply.
items.why: Meanders form on gentle gradients, not steep ones, and self-organise rather than dodging obstacles. Students project an intuitive 'water goes around things' model onto a fluid dynamic process.

items.q: What is lag time on a storm hydrograph?
items.correct: The time between peak rainfall and peak river discharge — affected by drainage basin shape, land use, soil saturation and vegetation.
items.misconception: Lag time is how long the storm itself lasts.
items.random_wrong: Lag time is the delay between thunder and lightning.
items.why: Students misread the graph. Lag time is the gap between maximum rain and maximum river flow — it tells you how quickly the basin responds, not the duration of rainfall.

items.q: What is hydraulic action in a river?
items.correct: The force of moving water itself compressing air into cracks in the river bank or bed, weakening rock until pieces break off — no sediment needed.
items.misconception: Hydraulic action is when rocks in the river smash into each other.
items.random_wrong: Hydraulic action is when the river dissolves rocks chemically.
items.why: Students mix up the four river erosion processes. Hydraulic action = water force; abrasion = water using rocks to scrape; attrition = rocks hitting rocks; solution (corrosion) = chemical dissolving.

items.q: Which African region suffers severe desertification?
items.why: It's Sahel. “Maghreb” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the maximum rainfall in hot deserts?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 250mm. “100mm” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What percentage of Northern Hemisphere is permafrost?
items.why: The real figure is 25 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
